speaker: interviewer
text: When we left off, the last tape, you had just met the woman who was to be your wife. And can you tell us a little bit more about your meeting, and your courtship, and your marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I went through the office from the mayor in Waldenburg, and I had a conversation with the secretary. I told her what I'm doing in Waldenburg. And I said, I have to have a paper. I'm going to Katowice, buying some leather merchandise for my shop. And they said it was very important to get that paper, otherwise they could grab me and take away the material, and send me to Warsaw to clean the [? grooves. ?] That's what they used to do in that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: To clean what, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [? grooves ?] from the-- from the bombing in Warsaw. The Germans, where they destroyed Warsaw. So it was very important to get for me that paper that I got a shop, and I went to buy merchandise. So I went to Katowice by train. I have to go all-- all day and at night to go by train, to come to Katowice. I came to Katowice, and the first thing, I went to that man what my sister was, with him, in business. And I told him she-- he said, it's not my fault. She got mad because I didn't ask what the brothers is doing, so she ran away. So he said, that's the reason he's not going to give any money. He was a very rich man. He was-- he was in concentration camp, in Auschwitz, with my sister, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally I settled with him. He gave me some money for her, and I went with that paper, that letter. My-- my sister was writing a letter to [? Lonia. ?] So when I came in that stall in that supermarket, she was with a-- with a partner, an older woman from the [? old ?] city. And they was with her sister. They was partners, and they got to this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in Polish clothing. I haven't got the money at that time to buy clothes for myself. So anyway, and I came, and I told her [? about ?] him. She-- she knew right away, because she recognized my face. It was like my sister's face. Anyway, she said to me she can't tell me anything what what-- what-- what she got to do, because her sister was [? out ?] [? of ?] [? town, ?] and when sister-- when her sister comes, then I'll meet, then we'll see. Anyway, I-- I bought a lot of leather, and I took it to my shop in back. And I said to my brother, I'm going to sell that leather right away, because I have to go back. So anyway, I went out, and I could have a million customers for that leather. And he said, well, we have to the use up the leather [? now. ?] I said-- I said, we can't do that. I'll bring-- I'll bring other leather.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, well, he's a partner. I took him in for a partner, because I got [? a ?] [? shop ?]. So anyway, he said, you didn't give me money [INAUDIBLE]. What kind of a partner you are? Anyway, I said, I'm going to sell [? F, ?] and [? now ?] [? that I ?] [? have an idea. ?] So-- and I went right back in a couple days. And I came there, her sister was already there, and we met.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the sister got a boyfriend, too, and so the boyfriend was there, and I am--

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you talking about your wife's sister, or your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- her name is Jean. Her name in Polish, it was [? Janka. ?] She's in New Jersey. She lives in New Jersey. Anyway, it's an older sister. Anyway, the guy-- when I came, she took me in the house. And when I came, there was sitting the other guy. He was-- he was a boyfriend to the elder-- older sister. And he-- when he [? Lonia, ?] he liked better [? Lonia ?] than her sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, what's the matter? Why you didn't pick her? I said, I didn't pick her. I just-- I-- it was-- it was made up in this-- in Auschwitz, that was talking about. [LAUGHS] So anyway, he was jealous because he want better-- she was looking better. Anyway, she went with me to Waldenburg right away. And she came to Waldenburg. Her sister was already-- How long did you know each other by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't know each other. Just one day, two days. So she agreed to go with me to Waldenburg. She wants to see what's going on there. She-- she thought I got a factory there. Anyway, the-- the-- the partner who was the older woman, se said, [? Lonia ?] is going to get married to a general. I was close. I was wearing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still in your uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was wearing a uniform, my uniform. It was an officer uniform, [DRINKS] because I made, for officers, boots. And an officer, a tailor, he said to me, I'll make you officer clothes, too, because you-- you-- you should wear officer clothes because you're [? dealing ?] [? with ?] an officer [? does ?] a lot. So whenever they saw me there, she was joking. [? Lonia-- ?] she said, [? Lonia ?] is going to get married with a general.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, she went with me to Waldenburg. When we came to Waldenburg my sister said, OK, that's it. So I like her when I saw her. Anyway, my sister said, we're going to make-- you know [? here, ?] but in Jewish they said, [? noyim. ?] You see, the first thing before we get married, that make a-- you know, with a rabbi. You make, like this, like engaged-- engagement. A betrothal. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we made a religious engagement with a-- with an older guy. You know, it was like a Orthodox. And she didn't-- she wasn't sure to get married. You know? Anyway, my sister, she-- she worked out the whole deal. And it was, maybe, about eight days or 10 days we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years before you actually got married had your sister planned this marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [LAUGHS] it was suddenly. Was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, she had planed it for a while, from what I understand, had she not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, this was in Auschwitz. They-- my-- my sister know my wife because she worked in the kitchen. My sister worked in the kitchen there, in Auschwitz, and my wife used to hang around by the kitchen. Maybe she'll see somebody who will bring her out something. So my sister liked her. She used to take her out something. But she liked her. She said, I got brothers. If I find a brother, I'll take you for sister-in-law in Auschwitz. So then she said to me, I've got a girl for you. And it was the right time, because in that time, she could get any boy. There was so many boys, and it was not too many boys came in uniform [? from ?] [? all ?] [? over ?], but the most. And girls, you didn't see many. So that's the thing. It took about a week or 10 days, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long have you been married now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 50 years. It was in the Jewish news, with our picture. 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your sister was right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Allen is-- is-- is 49.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the date of your marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date is-- is May the 26th-- 26th or 28th, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay with that shop? Did you say there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we was in that shop-- we got-- we got a couple places we was living, yeah. We was in that shop until the Jewish Federation gave me a notice. It's said, now there's going-- the last group is going over the Russian border. They're going-- I mean, the American side-- in the American side. So if you're-- then the Russians are going to close up, and you'll never get out from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they-- they took us to Israel. You know. The Jewish Federation was working very hard to get people to Israel. So we went-- we went from Salzburg. Salzburg, it was still on the Russian side. It was there that-- we came to Salzburg about six weeks. And then it was-- the last group went through the American zone. So we was-- we was on the train to get-- get through. All of a sudden, they cut off half train, and we didn't know we are on [? C ?] [? side ?] [? F. ?] That [? F ?] train went through, now it was-- was there. So it was the last group. We was afraid we can never get out from the Russian side. So finally, it was our [? F ?] train came to [? Landshut. ?] And it was on the fields there we was in [? Landshut. ?] It was very cold, wintertime.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they-- then they-- between [? Landshut ?] we came to Wasseralfingen, in the DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that? What part of Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is by-- by Stuttgart. By Stuttgart, around Stuttgart. So we came-- it was a new DP camp. We came in that DP camp, and when we went out from Waldenburg, so we have to walk the borders we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to what? I'm sorry. Could you repeat that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to walking-- the men. The men used to walk, and the women, they go by-- by-- by trucks. Because my wife is-- it was in the 15th month with Allen, with my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which month?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the fifth month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fifth month, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So actually the reason-- all the-- all the women were pregnant. The sister, too. Her sister, was-- too, the same thing. So we have to meet in Salzburg. So then, in Salzburg we went  to the [? Landshut. ?] From [? Landshut ?] we went to Wasseralfingen in the DP camp. And in the Wasseralfingen, we was there about three and a half years, almost.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: At?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- in Wasseralfingen, it was-- came in-- the DP camp, came in about 10 shoemakers, and they're making shoes. You see, we used to get food from the American government, you know, the DP. And when-- people was doing business on the side. You know? So it was-- it was butchers, it was tailors, it was-- and-- and I made boots, I made the shoe, I made the upper, I made the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, the shoemakers came in there-- haven't got an upper maker. So they said to me-- I knew the whole thing-- so they came to me, and they-- and they told me to stop making shoes, just make uppers for the shoemakers. I was there about three and a half years. And when it comes to-- to the time to go to America-- You left out something. Wait, we have to stop. Your son's birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my son-- my son was born in [? Allen. ?] This was the city from Wasseralfingen, that they knew as [? Allen. ?] He was born there. She was in fifth month when we came there. And then-- and he was two years and a couple months old when we started. We have to go-- we-- she's got relatives in New York. They sent the papers in 1940-- '46. And then the-- when the consul-- they run them to assign for-- for rooms-- for suite rooms for us in New York. And they were scared. They said, well, maybe we have to support then. They-- they refused, so nothing happened to the papers. So finally, the Jewish Federation find out. And we got-- they sent us papers from St. Louis, Illinois. We got papers to the Jewish Federation. They sent us papers. So we came-- we came to America straight to St. Louis in a little city of Belleville. We got a picture from the newspaper-- Belleville. And we-- we-- we was in Belleville a couple weeks, and they took us-- they-- it was their president from the Jewish Federation was a very rich man. Old man and his wife, old couple, they haven't got nobody. They wanted to adopt-- they want us to be there. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: They actually wanted to adopt you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they want. So I've got big-- big family in Cleveland, so I don't-- I didn't care for the adoption. I was sitting there in a hotel, and they took care-- they took care of us, everything, for two weeks. Then I-- I called up a cousin of mine, [? Harry ?] Singer, here in Cleveland. I said, I-- I want to come to-- to Cleveland. He said, what do you mean you want to come to Cleveland? Where are you calling? You are-- you are-- you are in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said no, I'm not in Poland, I'm in America. How do you came to America? He wants-- he wants-- I was talking-- send him a letter because I want papers from him. So I said to him, I want to come to Cleveland. I'm here in the city. The Jewish Federation took us over. So I even got his telegrams with everything. He said to me, I'm going to go to the Jewish Federation in Cleveland, and I'm going to see-- work out. So he came to the Jewish Federation. The Jewish Federation told him, he belongs there. They said, they took him over and they're responsible for him for two-- two years, so they're going to take care of them. So I said, I want him here. They said, you want him here? You have to be responsible for him, so you have to sign it. If you sign it here, we tell them down there to release him. So that's-- he signed it, and we came here to Cleveland. And what have you done here professionally? What has been your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came to Cleveland. He was-- he's got a-- he's got a furrier shop in downtown, and he was building houses with a partner. He said he wants me to be in the building. He said he wants me to start to paint. I said, I don't know if I like these kind of things to work by the buildings. He said, I got my trade. So he helped me to buy a shop. I bought a shop, and I took in new shoes and everything-- a repair shop-- and that's where I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're still there today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I got another shop. I got-- I got a shop here. I've got  a lot of shoes. I'm doing big business in-- and it was in-- in my new shop. My-- and I was there in that shop 30 years. And the old-- old shopping center, it got burned up. So I moved it here in [? Kona ?] [? Richmond ?] in Mayfield, and-- and I'm still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of your shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Stocks, S-T-O-C-K-S, Stocks Shoe Store.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you still working full time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I work from 10:00 to 6:00 every day. [LAUGHS] I do it for a hobby. I like to do it. It's not the money. It's just to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's talk about something really, really important-- the rest of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the names of your other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] My children. Allen, his name is [INAUDIBLE] after my father, and my youngest son is the name after of my mother. She was Feiga.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Freddy. In Jewish name, it's Ephraim. And my-- my daughter is after my wife's mother and father, Gloria [? Sherry. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandchildren, I've got-- the oldest is Elizabeth. You're going to see her, my daughter's daughter. And the-- the other one is Katherine, and Allen's is, you just talked to her here, Laura. And he's got a son, his name is Jonathan. And my son got two kids. He's got two daughters and a boy. The older daughter is Sarah, and then Jessica, and Zachary. These are the grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back over everything that happened, why do you think you came out of it OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Survived?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- just to tell the story about-- about my parents, about the whole family. I didn't feel that-- what I am-- I didn't feel I'm not going to die in-- in that time. I got a feeling I'm going to get through the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: To witness? So that you could witness? If you could give a message to the world, and to your family, what would it be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hope, that's a message. Just not to think back, to think about the future for the-- for the children and for the grandchildren. For the generations to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: That is beautiful, thank you. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Binstock, could you tell me who is in this picture, starting at the right to left? The first one is my father, and the second is my mother, and the third one is my mother's brother, and my-- my brother Berl, and my sister, Rivka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken when-- before I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- what year were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born, maybe, two years later, after the picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born in?

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was about 1909. And how did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was my brother from Israel, he sent me that picture, because my pictures was destroyed in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. From the right to the left, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Berl, my older brother, and my sister, Chaja and my uncle-- my mother's brother-- Hershel, and my sister, Bayla, and that's me in the end. And then is my sister, Rivka with my little sister, Sarah, and my-- my sister's daughter-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my sister's daughter-- what's the name-- is Ruchele. And my father, and my mother, and my sister Rivka's husband, and their little son, Jankele.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken, maybe, a year, a year and a half before the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother from-- from Israel sent me the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken in 1938, a year before the war. That's my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- that's from Israel, too, because my pictures was destroyed. I couldn't [? keep ?] [? it ?] [? up, ?] and they send me-- my brother sent it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Talk about him. Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my father when he was about 28 years old, the first one, and the second is my cousin here from Cleveland, and-- and the woman was my mother's aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this picture is taken in that time when he would have been-- he was 28 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is about what year, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's about-- it's about [? 108 ?] years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's an old picture. And did you also get this through Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, I got it in Cleveland from-- from his wife-- from that cousin, [? Harry ?] Singer, and his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Rivka. This picture, I got it here in Cleveland. This picture actually is made maybe 10 years before the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Losice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. From the right, who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older brother, Berl, and his wife and his daughter. And this picture was taken about 46-- 45 years ago, in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you give it-- get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother sent it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my younger sister, Chaja. She was-- this is the picture was made in Losice before the war. And she was in the partisan and got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kostopil.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when? About what year did she die?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- it was in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this picture, my brother from Israel sent it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my sister, Chaja. The picture was in Losice, next to our house. It was sitting on the wood. We used to use wood for-- for-- for warming up the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was maybe a year before the war broke out. It was 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Binstock, tell me about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Losice when the Germans occupied Losice. In the center, the [? left ?] group from Losice to-- to destroy them in Treblinka. That was in 19-- it was August, in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942. This picture-- this-- a Pollack took that picture, and he-- he sent it to a friend of mine, and that friend sent it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took all kind of picture in that time, the Pollacks. This is [? the ?] [? resting ?] spot of this group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you explain this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1943. I was-- I was running a shop to teach some boys-- they are like criminals, they took them out from the jails-- to teach-- to teach them a trade, and I was their teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly the name. It was a small little town close to a city in Russia, Saratov.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, I-- I stand next to that girl. She was a-- a teacher to sew-- to stitch-- you make uppers for the shoes, upper making.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Binstock, what is this about?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken from the newspaper, from the The-- the news man came in. We was just came in, in that city, in-- in that hotel. And [? they ?] [? fields ?] the newcomers in that city, so they took the picture and they write up a whole article about it. This was in-- in July the 7th.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: '47, Or later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we came to this country. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. and Mrs. Binstock, your whole family is gathered around you. Would you like to introduce them, Mrs. Binstock?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I will. Right here behind me, first row, is my daughter-in-law, Lisa. Here's Judy, my daughter-in-law, Allen's wife. Here we have Allen, my oldest son. Billy, my son-in-law, Gloria's husband. This is my daughter, Gloria, and her daughter, Elizabeth. Here is Laura, that's Allen's daughter. And here's Jonathan, Allen's son. Here we have Katie. That's my Gloria's younger daughter. Now we have here, in the front of me, here's my Sarah, Fred's daughter. And here's Zachary, Fred's son, and this is Jessica.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we forgot Fred.

speaker: interviewee
text: Went right by me.

speaker: interviewee
text: My Fred is next to his wife, Lisa. And now, let's not forget Jessica. This is Fred's younger daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do any of you have a message you'd like to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a short message. First of all, we'd like to thank God for all the blessings that you see before you right now. We feel blessed to be-- to be here, and together at this time. And just one brief note about what it means to be a survivor family, and that is that probably the greatest thing that was missing in our lives when my brother and my sister and I were growing up, was grandparents. And we feel extremely blessed that our children have known their grandparents. So we thank God for all of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do any of you kids have anything that you'd like to say? Well, thank you all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you.
